title: New exponent pairs, zero density estimates, and zero additive energy estimates: a systematic approach

def:5.summary: Phase function and model phase function: a smooth $F:[1,2]\to\mathbb{R}$ controlling the oscillation in van-der-Corput-type exponential sums $\sum_n e(F(n/N))$; 'model' phase functions are the monomial $x^{-c}$ family. The objects whose exponent pairs are catalogued.

def:7.summary: Additive energy $E_1(W)$ of a finite multiset $W\subset\mathbb{R}$: the number of quadruples $(t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4)\in W^4$ with $t_1+t_2=t_3+t_4$ (up to $o(1)$). The 'zero additive energy' quantity $A^*(\sigma)$ is the exponent of the additive energy of the $\sigma$-zeros of $\zeta$.

lem:13.summary: Exponent-pair processes: the set of exponent pairs $(k,\ell)$ is closed under the van der Corput $A$-process $(k,\ell)\mapsto(\tfrac{k}{2k+2},\tfrac{\ell}{2k+2}+\tfrac12)$ and $B$-process $(k,\ell)\mapsto(\ell-\tfrac12,k+\tfrac12)$ (plus convexity and the trivial pairs). The calculus that generates new exponent pairs from old.

conjecture:17.summary: The exponent pair conjecture: every point of the triangle $\{(k,\ell):0\le k\le\tfrac12\le\ell\le1,\,k+\ell\ge\tfrac12\}$ is an exponent pair; equivalently $\beta(\alpha)$ takes its conjectured piecewise-linear form. The target the $A/B$-process search approaches.

thm:20.summary: New exponent pairs: e.g. $(\tfrac{89}{1282},\tfrac{997}{1282})$ and $(\tfrac{652397}{9713986},\tfrac{7599781}{9713986})$ are exponent pairs — obtained by a systematic ($A/B$-process + recently established) search, improving the previously best known pairs.

thm:21.summary: The previously best (Bourgain-type) exponent pair $(\tfrac3{40},\tfrac{31}{40})$ — the benchmark thm:20 improves on, descending from Bourgain's $(\tfrac{13}{84},\tfrac{55}{84})$ via further processing.

def:22.summary: Growth rate $\mu(\sigma)$ of $\zeta$: the least exponent with $|\zeta(\sigma+it)|\ll|t|^{\mu(\sigma)+o(1)}$. Exponent pairs $(k,\ell)$ give $\mu(\tfrac12)\le\tfrac{k+\ell-1/2}{2}\cdots$; $\mu(\tfrac12)=0$ is the Lindelöf hypothesis.

thm:31.summary: $L^2$ mean value theorem for large values: for $\tfrac12\le\sigma\le1$, $\tau\ge0$, the large-value exponent satisfies $\mathrm{LV}(\sigma,\tau)\le\max(2-2\sigma,\,1+\tau-2\sigma)$. The Montgomery–Halász $MM^*$ baseline — sharp for small $\tau$, the starting point all improvements build on.

thm:32.summary: Bourgain's large values theorem (Bourgain 2000): given an exponent pair / exponential-sum input, $\mathrm{LV}(\sigma,\tau)$ is bounded by an expression interpolating the trivial bound and the gain from the exponential-sum estimate. The route by which exponent pairs (and Bourgain's ζ-curve decoupling) feed large-value bounds.

conjecture:30.summary: Montgomery's large value conjecture (in $\mathrm{LV}$ form): $\mathrm{LV}(\sigma,\tau)=\min(2-2\sigma,\tau)$ for all $\tfrac12<\sigma\le1$, $\tau\ge0$. The optimal large-value estimate; equivalent to $\|M_{\mathrm{Dir}}\|_{\infty\to q}\lesssim N+N^{1/2}T^{1/q}$.

def:37.summary: Zero density exponents: $N(\sigma,T)$ counts zeros $\rho$ of $\zeta$ with $\mathrm{Re}(\rho)\ge\sigma$, $|\mathrm{Im}(\rho)|\le T$; $A(\sigma)$ is the least exponent with $N(\sigma,T)\ll T^{A(\sigma)(1-\sigma)+o(1)}$. The quantity all zero-density bounds estimate.

conjecture:38.summary: The density hypothesis: $A(\sigma)\le2$ for all $\tfrac12\le\sigma<1$ (equivalently $N(\sigma,T)\ll T^{2(1-\sigma)+o(1)}$). Would give primes in intervals $x^{1/2+\epsilon}$; implied by Lindelöf and by Montgomery's conjecture.

thm:45.summary: Montgomery's large value conjecture implies the density hypothesis. A clean implication tying the LV pipeline to the zero-density target.

thm:46.summary: Ingham–Halász: the Lindelöf hypothesis implies the density hypothesis (and more). The classical sufficient condition.

thm:47.summary: Ingham's zero-density bound (1940): $A(\sigma)\le\tfrac3{2-\sigma}$ for $\tfrac12<\sigma<1$. The first nontrivial zero-density estimate; the floor everything improves on near $\sigma=\tfrac12$.

thm:48.summary: Huxley's zero-density bound: $A(\sigma)\le\tfrac3{3\sigma-1}$ for $\tfrac12<\sigma<1$; in particular the density hypothesis holds for $\sigma\ge\tfrac56$. The pre-Guth–Maynard state of the art for $\sigma$ near $\tfrac34$.

thm:49.summary: Guth–Maynard zero-density bound: $A(\sigma)\le\tfrac{15}{3+5\sigma}$ for $\tfrac12<\sigma<1$ — at $\sigma=\tfrac34$ this is $\tfrac{30}{13}<\tfrac{12}{5}$ (Huxley), the first improvement in the relevant range in decades. Imported from Guth–Maynard's new large value estimate; the input behind thm:50.

thm:50.summary: Improved Heath-Brown zero-density theorem: $A(\sigma)\le\tfrac3{10\sigma-7}$ for $\tfrac7{10}<\sigma\le1$. The paper's new bound for $\sigma$ near $\tfrac34$ — sharper than both Huxley and Guth–Maynard there — obtained by feeding the new large-value/additive-energy estimates through the Heath-Brown relation.

thm:51.summary: Improved Bourgain density-hypothesis bound: for $\tfrac{17}{22}\le\sigma\le\tfrac45$, $A(\sigma)\le\max(\tfrac2{9\sigma-6},\tfrac9{8(2\sigma-1)})$ — in particular pushes the range where the density hypothesis is known down from $\sigma\ge\tfrac56$ (Huxley) toward $\sigma\ge\tfrac{17}{22}$ in part.

thm:52.summary: Bourgain's zero-density theorem from exponent pairs (Bourgain 1995): for an exponent pair $(k,\ell)$ with $k<\tfrac15$, $\ell>\tfrac35$, $15\ell+20k>13$, one gets an explicit $A(\sigma)$ bound. The mechanism turning new exponent pairs (thm:20) into new zero-density bounds.

thm:53.summary: Optimised Bourgain zero-density bound: combining thm:52 with the new exponent pairs gives, e.g. $A(\sigma)\le\tfrac{11}{12(4\sigma-3)}$ for $\tfrac34<\sigma\le\tfrac{14}{15}$. The exponent-pair route's contribution to the updated $A(\sigma)$ landscape.

thm:63.summary: The Heath-Brown relation (1979): if $(\sigma,\tau,\rho,\rho^*,s)$ lie in the admissible set, then $\rho^*\le1-2\sigma+\tfrac12\max(\rho,\dots)$ — the inequality converting a large-value exponent $\rho=\mathrm{LV}$ and an additive-energy exponent $\rho^*$ into a zero-density bound. The hinge between the LV/additive-energy machinery and $A(\sigma)$.

thm:64.summary: New additive energy estimates: for $\tfrac7{10}\le\sigma<1$, bounds on $A^*(\sigma)(1-\sigma)$ (e.g. $\le\max(\tfrac{18-19\sigma}{2(3\sigma-\dots)},\dots)$ for $\tfrac34\le\sigma\le\tfrac56$). The additive energy of the $\sigma$-zeros of $\zeta$, controlled for the first time in this range — the input that, via thm:63, yields the improved zero-density bound thm:50.

edges.note: New exponent pairs are produced by a systematic search over compositions of the $A$- and $B$-processes.
The new pairs sharpen the previously best Bourgain-type pair $(\tfrac3{40},\tfrac{31}{40})$.
Advances toward the exponent pair conjecture.
Bounds on the $\zeta$-growth exponent $\mu(\sigma)$ are read off from exponent pairs.
Bourgain's large-value theorem takes an exponential-sum / $\mu(\sigma)$ input and outputs a bound on $\mathrm{LV}(\sigma,\tau)$.
Interpolates against the $L^2$ mean-value baseline.
Establishes Montgomery's large-value conjecture in the small-$\tau$ range.
The new additive-energy estimates are derived from the large-value theorems.
Bounds the additive energy $A^*(\sigma)$ of the $\sigma$-zeros.
The improved zero-density bound is obtained by feeding the large-value and additive-energy exponents through the Heath-Brown relation.
Uses the new additive-energy estimates as the $\rho^*$ input to the Heath-Brown relation.
Sharper than the Guth–Maynard bound for $\sigma$ near $\tfrac34$ (the regime $\sigma>\tfrac7{10}$).
Sharper than Huxley's bound in the same range.
Guth–Maynard's bound beats Huxley's near $\sigma=\tfrac34$.
Huxley's bound beats Ingham's for $\sigma$ not too close to $\tfrac12$.
Optimised by inserting the new exponent pairs into Bourgain's exponent-pair zero-density mechanism.
Uses the new exponent pairs.
The exponent-pair zero-density mechanism runs on the resulting $\mu(\sigma)$ bounds.
Derived from Bourgain-type large-value bounds in the relevant $\sigma$-window.
Assumes Montgomery's conjecture to derive the density hypothesis.
Shows the density hypothesis would follow.
Lindelöf likewise implies the density hypothesis.
States a bound on the zero-density exponent $A(\sigma)$.
The Guth–Maynard large-value estimate underlies the new additive-energy bounds in the overlapping regime.

exponent_pairs.description: The exponent-pair calculus (phase functions, A/B processes), the exponent-pair conjecture, and the new pairs found by systematic search.

zeta_growth.description: The growth exponent $\mu(\sigma)$ for $\zeta(\sigma+it)$, derived from exponent pairs.

large_values.description: Large-value theorems for Dirichlet polynomials: the $L^2$ mean-value baseline, Bourgain's theorem from exponential-sum inputs, and Montgomery's conjecture.

zero_density.description: Zero-density exponents $A(\sigma)$: the density hypothesis, the classical bounds (Ingham, Huxley), Guth–Maynard, and the paper's new improvements via the Heath-Brown relation and via optimised exponent pairs.

additive_energy.description: Zero additive energy estimates $A^*(\sigma)$ — the new ingredient feeding the Heath-Brown relation.
